posts.name: Nigel Bruce
posts.cooked: It seems the 2019 Clos des Lambrays has been released at double its 2018 price; perhaps it was only a matter of time before LVMH matched the pricing of its neighbour Clos de Tart. Jacques Devauges seems to have undertaken big changes there. Neal Martin was very impressed.

posts.name: Mike Wenzel
posts.cooked:
What’s it going for? I have four bottles of the 2002 purchased from Garagiste for $54.99.

Guessing it is considerably more than that.

Dear Friends,

If you were considering purchasing any 2002 Burgundy, now is the time.

One of our best Burgundy deals ever – Grand Cru examples do not come at this price, especially in 2002. This wine is a classic for the vintage and it should age beautifully for many years. Due to limited quantities that’s all I will say. We should have the lowest price in the world.

This offer is for current email list members only:

2002 Clos de Lambrays Grand Cru - $54.99 (BH92) STRICT LIMIT 12/person
(compare at up to $100 – this is not the regular 1er or Morey St. Denis)

Burghound: “(produced from two large parcels of differing vine age - one that is approximately two-thirds of the blend and is now 45 years of age and a second, smaller group of vines that is approximately 20 years of age). Medium ruby. Aromas of wild berries, game and a subtle note of smoke lead to rich, intense, moderately fat flavors that are wonderfully well balanced largely because of the racy, indeed bright acidity. This has tightened up considerably since it was bottled with a dusty, sweet and precise finish that is strikingly long. In short, delicious yet entirely serious and a wine that should age gracefully for two decades, perhaps longer. 92/2012+”
That’s just plain bananas…I’d backfill the 2005 at that price.

I recently purchased the incredibly rare Taupenot Merme Clos des Lambrays… the neighbors must be livid that they’re holding them back from being a true monopole.

posts.name: Mike Wenzel
posts.cooked: Sorry was not clear. That 2002 offer was from way back in 2005. The good old days.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: I’m not sure there’s a world in which I would pay over $200 for current vintage Lambrays no matter the yield when back vintages can be found for not much more. Especially at the 2019 pricing YEESH.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’m not sure there’s a world in which I would pay over $200 for current vintage Lambrays no matter the yield when back vintages can be found for not much more. Especially at the 2019 pricing YEESH.

The shift is indeed aggressive but it’s worth saying that they are making real improvements, with the aspiration to brining the quality up to the level it once routinely attained (ie 1971 and before, and especially the interwar years). In the last couple of decades, the deal was you could buy something called a grand cru, for the price of a premier cru, that tasted like a village wine.

link_counts.title: I know it’s not “the market” but damn Burgundy pricing…

posts.name: Nigel Bruce
posts.cooked: Ouch, William… any exceptions to that rule of thumb?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Ouch, William… any exceptions to that rule of thumb?

To me, the wines took a step up with 09, 10, 12, 15… but, some of these vintages, having tasted great in barrel, developed brett in bottle.

All in all, I think that, by virtue of being a very charming guy and prices being very “inexpensive for a grand cru”, Thierry Brouin and the estate’s owners were given a pass for overcropping, planting poor quality clones/selections of Pinot Noir, formulaic vinification (huge tanks, and rotofermenters for the lower appellations), and sloppy élevage. Wines such as 1919, 1923, 1934, 1937 and 1947 Clos des Lambrays can compete with anything in Burgundy, and that should be what the estate aspires to achieve!

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
We used to receive many wines from Domaine Taupenot Merme - including his rare C d Lambrays - in Quebec, Canada plus his others g-crus ( for example : Taupenot-Merme - Corton Rognet and Charme ).

For some unknown reason and for the last many years no more CdLambrays by Taupenot Merme but we still receive his Charmes and Corton Rognet - which stay on the shelves for a long time.

We could still buy his Charme from vintage 2011 ( at $169 ) up to vintage 2017 ( at $ 312 ) except for vintage 2013 and 2015.

Same story for his Corton Rognet. We could still back-fill many older vintages at the release prices including the 2015. .

I kept a record of the released price as an indicator for my personal use whenever I wish to consider to back-fill.
Ccrton Rognet 17 ($261 )
Corton Rognet 16 ($231 )
Corton Rognet 15 ($203 )
Corton Rognet 14 ($181 )
Corton Rognet 11 ($141 )
